title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What Is Foreign Direct Investment?
Ever since 1991, when the Indian economy opened its doors to the world, the country’s investment climate has shown considerable improvements. The ease of Foreign Direct Investment rules can be considered the primary attribute of this growth. The financial year 2021-22¹, however, has been remarkable as the total FDI rose by 2%, which is the highest ever. The inflow this year stands at $81.97 billion, which was $74.39 and $62 billion in 2019-20 and 2018-192, respectively.
While these trends are indeed an endorsement of India’s status as a sought-after destination for investors all across the globe, it is important to understand what is FDI and all its important aspects. Read on to learn all about foreign direct investment.
What is a foreign direct investment (FDI)?
Foreign Direct Investment, better known as FDI3, is when a foreign entity owns or controls stakes in the shares of a firm or company in another country. In simple words, it is a kind of investment that is made by an individual or a company in one country into a business interest which is located in another country. A major driver of economic growth, FDI acts as a vital channel of directly made investments between two countries.
How does FDI benefit India? 7
Being a fast-growing economy, India needs money for its growth. The taxes the government collects are not enough and can bring the country into a budget deficit. An increase in inflation directly results from more money printing, and thus it cannot be a solution. If the government borrows too much from foreign countries, it can increase the country’s debt, lowering the credit rating. In such a scenario, foreign direct investment is an ideal way to have a monetary flow without a debt burden.  The money that comes in is further utilised by the government for infrastructural development, increasing exports, and employment generation.
The stimulation of the economy encourages healthy inter-industry competition, where the customer, in turn, benefits. There is an upgradation of the knowledge scale as innovations are implemented. A wider skill set also reaches the labour class, honing their talents.
FDI, thus has been a boon for the Indian economy. The benefits of FDI for India, therefore, cannot be understated.
What are the FDI routes in India?
There are 2 main FDI routes in India2:
Automatic FDI Route In the automatic route, a non-resident or an Indian company does not require primary government or RBI permission to conduct any foreign investments. For example:
Medical devices insurance
Power exchanges
Thermal power
Insurance The automatic route is approved up to a certain percentage.
Government FDI RouteThe Government FDI Route makes it mandatory for the foreign entity to get approval from the Indian government. The Foreign Investment Facilitation Portal accepts applications with a single-window clearance. The applications are assessed and approved by the Ministry of Commerce.
Sectors prohibited from FDI in India4
Despite its advantages, there are certain sectors where FDI is prohibited. These are:
Agricultural activities
Atomic energy
Lotteries
Chit Funds
Tobacco industry
Real estate and housing
Transferable Development Rights.
Significant developments in FDI
The effect of FDI in India has been significant in many ways. Let us take a look:
The Ed-Tech firm Byju’s raised $500 million in September 2020 in Silver Lake-led funding.5
Unacedemy raised $150 million in the same niche with SoftBank Group from Japan.6
7.73% of the Jio Platform by Reliance was picked up by Google for $4.5 billion. This has been one of the biggest such deals in the history of the Indian corporate industry.8
One of USA’s reputed equity firms, General Atlantic, in June 2020, spent over $900 million to buy a share in the Reliance-owned Jio Platform.9
In August 2021, Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners invested a $100 million in the Indian renewable energy market.10
India also made an agreement with the United Kingdom in September 2021 to promote an enhanced business partnership. 11
Conclusion
As more and more global entities participate in the domestic market, it is the Indian consumer that would benefit from the increasing competition. It is also estimated that the fusion of foreign direct investment with flagship initiatives like Stand Up India and Make in India will encourage more foreign inventors and further boost the already flourishing Indian economy.
